data.title: I'm Building Income With The Most Essential Businesses In The World
data.description:
Introduction (A History & Supply Chain Lesson)

Investing during times of war is tough, as we are bombarded with daily headlines that make it nearly impossible to estimate how long it will take and how bad it will get. By the time I’m done writing this article, the market could be 5% lower or 5% higher, depending on what kind of news has hit the wire.

And, to be honest, as annoying as it may be, I’m writing this from a safe office, far away from any rocket impacts, which means my complaints are truly only about my new workload. Unlike some of my friends, I don’t have to be close to a bomb shelter.

The reason I’m telling you all of this is that I spent the past few days thinking about how this war, like almost any other war, is massively impacting supply chains and showing the world just how important prudent supply management is. While I’m painting with a broad brush, I think it’s fair to say that almost any major war in history was either about supply chains or decided about supply chains, or both.

Personally, I love World War II history, especially when it’s explained by people like Victor Davis Hanson, who does a great job of highlighting the mechanics behind the war machinery. For example, Japan moved into the Dutch East Indies in World War II to secure oil and rubber. Some say that if they hadn’t dragged the U.S. into the war by bombing Pearl Harbor, they would have been in a great (much better) spot to gain more territory and keep it. Once the U.S. got involved, Japan was quickly overwhelmed by its massive industrial power. Unlike nations like Germany, the U.S. was able to boost military output without having its factories bombed.

That’s also why Iwo Jima was so important. Although Japan was almost defeated, and Iwo Jima is one of the smallest islands in the area, it was critical for aircraft. By capturing it, the U.S. could fly much bigger bombing missions in Japan.

And then there’s Germany, which was forced to split its troops in 1942 during its Soviet Union invasion. They had to capture Russia’s Baku oil fields. It would both cut off Russian supply and provide German forces with enough fuel to continue. They failed.

Fast-forward to 2026, and it seems that every time a new conflict happens, the market finds out just how critical supply chains are and that while software and tech companies may have been the biggest growth drivers in recent years, when things get bad, it’s all about food and energy security. This is what the spike in fertilizer prices looked like:

In a recent article, a reader who you may know as “Geeky Mechanic” wrote for Main Street Alpha (my service comes to Seeking Alpha on April 7) about how critical this situation has become and why the U.S. is a standout here:

In the quest for food security, I think we will see an increasing number of trade deals and investments globally around the needed commodities, whether fertilizers or directly concerning specific agricultural goods. The U.S. stands to benefit in the long term as investments ramp up in the sector and global supply chains move to prevent any future disruptions. Other countries will also look to whoever can supply them to buy either fertilizers or finished agricultural goods. U.S. agricultural exports are already valued at $120 billion in yearly exports, which I think will continue its upward trend, and might even accelerate. - Main Street Alpha (Geeky Mechanic).

Moreover, because the Strait of Hormuz ships roughly a fifth of all seaborne oil and a quarter of global LNG, these commodities suddenly became scarce, which led to full-blown supply shortages in markets like Southeast Asia.

And although the U.S. also saw weakness in stock prices, declining investor sentiment, and higher recession odds, I think it’s more than fair to make the case that it has the resources to gain a competitive advantage. I discussed this in an article a few years ago titled “Red, White, And Wealth: Building An All-American Dividend Portfolio.”

• America has access to highly valuable energy commodities and minerals.• America has sufficient freshwater and fertile ground.• America has the world’s largest defense force to protect all of it.

I’m painting with a broad brush, but I think you get the idea.

Every time a conflict happens (think of the Ukraine and the Iran War) or a major disruption like the pandemic, the market is, once again, confronted by the harsh reality that our economies truly depend on physical supply chains. We learn that farmers don’t magically grow crops, that gasoline depends on complex networks, and that even things like plastic and helium are extremely critical.

That’s what this article is about, as I’ll highlight a number of terrific companies with critical operations in some of the world’s most important supply chains. Not only are these companies important right now, but I think a diversified basket of these stocks will generate elevated long-term returns. Even better, they come with either dividend growth or income.

So, as we have a lot more to discuss, let’s dive into the second part of this article.

The "Fortress Portfolio" - Critical Supply Chain Dividend Stocks

I don’t like event-driven investing. That’s why I don’t buy energy stocks just to benefit from a war. I owned these stocks before they happened. It’s for the same reason I don't always cancel my insurance the moment I expect risks of something bad happening are low.

The thing is that I want to own high-quality stocks that both shield me against mayhem and allow me to build wealth over time. Everything else doesn’t work for me, as my goal is to beat the market over time. That won’t work when I’m knee-deep into stocks that only perform well when global geopolitical risks explode. Can you imagine how poor my performance would be over time?

• Energy & Midstream

This one is the most obvious in light of what’s going on in Iran and the Middle East, in general. America has the Permian Basin, while Canada has the Western Canadian Sedimentary Basin. Both areas have deep reserves, low breakeven prices, and they are shielded against rocket strikes and other things that currently hurt OPEC’s output.

In this area, I want to be “always” long the most efficient players, including Diamondback Energy (FANG), Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ), larger players like Exxon Mobil (XOM), and royalty-focused players like Viper Energy (VNOM). This basket has a variable dividend with a yield of roughly 5-6% at current oil prices (that’s back-of-the-napkin math).

Moreover, as I know that some readers dislike the dependence on oil prices, midstream companies are also a great way to buy income and safety, as these players own the critical infrastructure needed to process and ship the commodities produced by the aforementioned companies. Think of ONEOK (OKE), Kinder Morgan (KMI), Antero Midstream (AM), TC Energy (TRP), and Enbridge (ENB). All of these stocks are C-Corps that do not issue K-1 forms. If you don’t mind K-1s (or even like them), my favorites are Western Midstream (WES) and MPLX (MPLX). This basket should pay investors roughly 5-7% without elevated risks.

Essentially, the energy stocks I just gave you (upstream and midstream) give you perfect coverage of the entire North American energy industry if you were to buy all of them.

• Agriculture

Agriculture is a tough topic. On the one hand, it’s ultra critical, as we all need to eat. It’s even more important than housing, as even the homeless need to eat. On the other hand, agricultural goods, in general, tend to be very abundant. Fertilizer, for example, is a commodity group that mostly consists of potash, phosphates, and nitrogen. These are very abundant in the world, which means companies that produce these goods often rely on their own ability to produce more cheaply than others and supply/pricing shocks to grow.

In general, the industry tends to run on thin margins, as the overview below shows. Crop prices usually barely trade above total input costs, as it’s such a homogenous market.

One example is Mosaic (MOS), the American giant with major exposure to potash and phosphates. On a long-term basis, even if we include dividends (total return), we see that there has been no real shareholder value creation. The main added value seems to be inflation protection during periods when energy and crop prices soar.

And honestly, that works. Many make the case that we need to own some crops to protect against inflation. I disagree, as I think long-term investing in futures comes with unnecessary added costs like rollovers. Mosaic at least has a dividend of 3.5%. 2-3% exposure in a portfolio for added inflation protection makes a lot of sense in my book.

Other picks are Nutrien (NTR), which I sometimes call the “Canadian Mosaic,” as they often move in lockstep. Nutrien yields 2.9%.

Then there’s Deere & Company (DE), which is the only high-margin agriculture stock on my radar, as it sells high-end tractors and machinery with substantial pricing power. As its chart shows (it shows the ratio between Deere and the S&P 500), it has consistently generated shareholder value, making it a terrific dividend growth stock for most investors looking to buy some industrial exposure.

The downside is that Deere yields just 1.2%. Luckily, it has a 5-year dividend CAGR of 15.3% that far outpaces the dividend growth of higher-yielding industrial stocks.

I also want to bring up Archer-Daniels-Midland (ADM) here. This company is ultra critical to the global food supply chain, as it processes, transports, and distributes crops like corn and wheat, produces ethanol, food-related products, and manages silos, rail networks, and countless other assets that farmers and food companies rely on.

ADM yields slightly less than 3.0% and is looking at a rebound after three years of double-digit EPS contraction. In 2026 and 2027, analysts expect 20% and 19% EPS growth, respectively. I think it is able to beat these numbers if we get inflationary tailwinds. In general, I believe that due to the surge in energy prices and shifting demand to North American exports, this company could be a surprisingly good stock over the next few years.

Then there’s logistics.

• Logistics

Supply chains are worthless without efficient transportation. You cannot get fertilizers, energy, people, or anything else from Point A to Point B without reliable transportation. The Strait of Hormuz is a great example of that.

Here, I like all North American Class I railroads, as they all have wide moat business models, a focus on dividend growth, buybacks, and healthy balance sheets. However, two stand out today, which are Union Pacific (UNP) and the Canadian National Railway (CNI).

Union Pacific yields 2.3% with a track record of 19 consecutive annual dividend hikes. The company is in the process of merging with Norfolk Southern (NSC). While they are waiting for approval (which could get rejected), I am very upbeat, as even the president has made clear that he sees a strategic benefit in what could be the first transcontinental railroad in the U.S.

A strategic network like this would make it even easier to connect demand across the country and de-risk supply chains. For example, let’s say ports in the West struggle with capacity problems. Other ports could take over, as fast railroad connections could help with cross-country shipments. Especially in emergency situations, that’s valuable.

Canadian National yields 2.6% with a 5-year CAGR of 13.7%. It’s critical, as more than 65% of all traffic originates on its network, which connects 7 major ports across two nations. This network includes the corridor down to Louisiana, China (the biggest rail market), and both Canadian coasts.

These assets aren’t just critical, but they also have the ability to generate consistent shareholder value. Especially if I’m right about growth broadening, I think we’ll find alpha here.

Another honorable mention is Prologis (PLD). This is the world’s biggest industrial REIT with a focus on high-end warehouses that cater to some of the biggest consumer-focused companies on the planet, all of which need smooth value chains. This includes Amazon (AMZN). Currently, PLD yields 3.3% with a 5-year dividend CAGR of 11.6%.

As goods need warehouses and our modern economy relies on these supply chains, PLD is highly critical, and one of the few REITs I would literally call “mission-critical.”

• Chemicals

I’m almost at this point again, where I realize I am writing a way-too-long article. However, I need to mention Linde (LIN) and Air Products & Chemicals (APD), as two chemical giants that provide a number of critical products, including helium. Linde, for example, produces helium in places like Kansas and stores it in Texas. Especially in current times, we’re reminded just how important these companies are for our economies.

Linde yields 1.3% with a 5-year dividend CAGR of 9.1%. For APD, these numbers are 2.5% and 6.0%, respectively (higher yield, yet slower growth).

On a rather consistent basis, LIN has outperformed APD, as the LIN/APD total return ratio below shows. Going forward, I’m bullish on both, especially due to my cyclical value thesis.

I have to say that all of these companies are so fascinating that I could easily write 2,000+ words on each of them. And maybe, going forward, I will do just that.

But for now, here’s the takeaway:

Takeaway

On a rather frequent basis, wars remind us of how important (and fragile) global supply chains are. While I am biased due to my background that includes a supply chain-focused master’s degree, I am convinced that understanding supply chains is extremely critical.

Today, I gave you my take on some of the most critical companies, players that not only support our economies, but also have the ability to add tremendous value to portfolios and come with either decent income or consistent dividend growth.

None of these companies is likely to be replaced. They have no competition from AI, and, if I’m right about the rebound of the “cyclical economy,” I think the next few years will be very bullish for all of them.

So, stay tuned, as I’ll give these companies (and others) a lot more attention.

data.title: 2 ETFs I'm Dollar-Cost Averaging Into As The Case For A Bear Market Increases
data.description:
Introduction

In recent weeks, the Technology (XLK) sector has continued to slide where it entered into correction territory. In my opinion, this has a lot to do with the ongoing conflict in the Middle East.

While I expect more volatility in the near-term despite the Nasdaq (NDAQ) surging on potential news of the war ending soon, I'm continuing to dollar-cost average into some of my favorite growth ETFs.

Going forward, I remain bullish on artificial intelligence and anticipate growth-focused ETFs see a strong run after the conflict with Iran gets resolved. But in the short to medium-term, these funds could see muted returns.

In this article, I discuss the two ETFs I'm buying currently, and why both are attractive if you're focused on growth and have a long-term time horizon.

Tech Stocks Headed For A Bear Market?

Coming into 2026, the broader market had solid momentum after closing up more than 16% in 2025. Rate cut hopes were on the table. But now with the ongoing war and President Trump threatening to blow up Iran's oil wells if they fail to make a deal, we could see a bear market soon.

Whether the two sides can come to an agreement remains to be seen, but investors are growing worried as the days go on. Currently, it appears many are raising cash. And while the market seems to be in oversold territory, I think we see a steeper sell-off if we do end up continuing operations in Iran.

The main reason is because we are likely to see higher inflation. Coming into the new year, the market was pricing in interest rate cuts. Now, there's a potential to see rate hikes. If we do see a hike, I believe this will send the market into bear territory. But of course, a lot can change in a short amount of time.

My suggestion is investors have some cash readily available to take advantage of the buying opportunities I anticipate in the near to medium term. While I'm currently buying the two ETFs I discuss today in small batches, I would load up should we see a bear market.

#1 Schwab US Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG)

First ETF I'm buying is SCHG. As a result of the Technology sector entering into correction territory, the ETF has underperformed year-to-date. SCHG is down double-digits compared to the S&P (SP500), who's down roughly 7%.

SCHG has 45% exposure to the Technology sector with behemoths like NVIDIA (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), Broadcom (AVGO), Alphabet (GOOGL), META Platforms (META), and Amazon (AMZN) making up its top 10 holdings.

But as you can see from the chart below, all have underperformed significantly year-to-date, down double-digits at the time of writing. In my opinion, this has nothing to do with the companies' fundamentals but more to do with the strong performances they saw in the years prior.

When stocks see a strong rally, they're likely to see periods of underperformance afterward. As an investor, focusing on the fundamentals and long-term performance is key.

While past performance is no indicator of future performance, SCHG's long-term track record speaks for itself. And this is why it's prudent to zoom out. Since its inception on December 11, 2009, SCHG has outperformed the largest S&P 500 ETF, (VOO) in total returns, led by its higher exposure to Technology.

Here's a break down of the fund's annualized performance since 2015 until 2024. As you can see below, 2023 was their strongest year so far with over 50% returns. In 2025, SCHG closed up 17.63% compared to 16.65% for the S&P.

I'm also a fan of SCHG's more concentrated portfolio with 196 holdings compared to VOO's over 500. While the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ) outperformed SCHG pretty significantly, SCHG's better price point and lower expense ratio are reasons I prefer the ETF.

Going forward, I think AI will continue to shape the economic landscape. In the near term, AI stocks could see volatility as fears of labor market disruption could weigh on the performance of tech stocks and funds alike.

But long-term, I believe the rapid adoption of AI will benefit SCHG and peers alike. And as a result, SCHG will likely see strong returns.

#2 Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (CGDV)

As the name implies, CGDV provides investors with income. But something I like about CGDV is although the fund focuses on dividends, it acts like a growth fund.

This is because the ETF focuses on value investing. CGDV looks for undervalued stocks based in the U.S. who have the potential to see strong capital appreciation long-term.

Probably my favorite thing about CGDV is their multi-manager approach. The ETF currently has three portfolio managers, each focusing on a specific sleeve of the portfolio.

In my opinion, this gives them a higher chance of outperforming long-term despite their shorter track record. CGDV has an inception date of June 24, 2025. So, quite different from SCHG.

SCHG is passively-managed, which is why their expense ratio is low at just 0.04%. CGDV is also reasonable at 0.33%, solid for an actively-managed fund.

Another key difference is SCHG undergoes a full reconstitution annually. CGDV's managers offload stocks whenever they see fit because of its actively-managed, multi-manager strategy.

CGDV has decent exposure to the Technology sector for a dividend-focused fund. Currently, the fund has 12.6% exposure. Their top sector is the Industrial (XLI) sector followed by Financials (XLF), Technology, and then Healthcare (XLV).

I believe CGDV's current exposure give them a perfect blend of growth and income as well as downside protection should we see a prolonged bear market. This is because of the exposure to Healthcare, which accounts for 11.2% of the portfolio.

Below are their current top 10 holdings:

Year-to-date, CGDV has experienced less of a drawdown compared to other popular ETFs. SCHG has seen the largest decline, driven by its high exposure to Technology stocks.

SCHG's sister fund, the Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) has performed well, led by its exposure to the Energy (XLE) sector. While capital appreciation is important for a lot of investors, seeing less of a pullback is also something to pay close attention to as this can speak to a fund's quality.

Since inception, CGDV managed to outperform VOO and SCHG in total returns despite their higher exposure to tech. This speaks to their quality despite having only 9 months public.

Why These Two ETFs

In my opinion, SCHG & CGDV give investors the perfect blend of growth and income. Furthermore, both take different approaches with SCHG's passively-managed style and CGDV's multi-manager, active approach to value investing.

Both are core positions in my portfolio and offer manageable overlap at 31%. SCHG gives me the largest exposure to stocks poised to benefit from AI. They also offer a low-cost structure and a proven long-term track record.

CGDV's track record is significantly shorter. But I believe their active approach with having multiple portfolio managers gives them an edge over competitors.

Their capital appreciation first approach also make them the perfect ETF to hold as a core holding and build individual income positions around.

Both SCHG & CGDV also pay tax-favorable distributions despite having lower yields. This makes them desirable for holding inside taxable accounts.

But as previously mentioned, both funds could see underperformance risks despite me being bullish on the future of AI and strong past performances. Particularly, if we do end up deploying ground troops, which is think at the moment is unlikely.

But long-term, I think the brief correction in tech stocks make them even more attractive. So, if you're looking for growth-focused ETFs that have the potential to make great long term core holdings for a growth-centric portfolio, then consider both SCHG and CGDV.
